stem: The equation \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \) has complex conjugate characteristic roots \( \alpha \pm i\omega \), giving an oscillatory solution \( e^{\alpha t}\left(c_1\cos(\omega t) + c_2\sin(\omega t)\right) \). What is the angular frequency \( \omega \)?
solution: The roots are \( r = \frac{-([[b]]) \pm \sqrt{([[b]])^{2} - 4([[c]])}}{2} \). Since \( ([[b]])^{2} - 4([[c]]) < 0 \), the imaginary part is \( \omega = \frac{\sqrt{4([[c]]) - ([[b]])^{2}}}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the division by 2, giving twice the frequency.
This reports \( \omega^{2} \) rather than \( \omega \).
This gives the real part \( |\alpha| \) instead of the frequency.
This uses the constant coefficient \( [[c]] \) directly.
This is one more than the frequency.
This is one less than the frequency.
This is two more than the frequency.

stem: The equation \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \) has complex conjugate characteristic roots \( \alpha \pm i\omega \), giving the solution \( e^{\alpha t}\left(c_1\cos(\omega t) + c_2\sin(\omega t)\right) \). What is the exponential rate \( \alpha \) that governs whether solutions grow or decay?
solution: The characteristic roots are \( r = \frac{-([[b]]) \pm \sqrt{([[b]])^{2} - 4([[c]])}}{2} \), so the real part is \( \alpha = \frac{-([[b]])}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign from the real part.
This forgets to divide by 2.
This uses the coefficient \( [[b]] \) directly as \( \alpha \).
This gives the angular frequency \( \omega \) instead of the decay rate \( \alpha \).
This is one more than the correct rate.
This is one less than the correct rate.
This is two more than the correct rate.

stem: The equation \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \) has complex conjugate characteristic roots \( \alpha \pm i\omega \), so its oscillatory factor \( \cos(\omega t) \) and \( \sin(\omega t) \) repeat with period \( T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} \). What is \( T \)?
solution: From the roots \( \alpha \pm i\omega \), the angular frequency is \( \omega = \frac{\sqrt{4([[c]]) - ([[b]])^{2}}}{2} \), so the period is \( T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the factor of 2 in the numerator.
This uses \( 4\pi \) instead of \( 2\pi \) in the numerator.
This multiplies by \( \omega \) instead of dividing.
This divides by the constant coefficient \( [[c]] \) instead of \( \omega \).
This adds an extra factor of \( \pi \) to the period.
This is half the correct period.
This is twice the correct period.

stem: The undamped oscillator \( y'' + [[omega]]^{2}y = 0 \) has purely imaginary characteristic roots \( \pm [[omega]]i \), giving solution \( y(t) = c_1\cos([[omega]]t) + c_2\sin([[omega]]t) \). Given \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) and \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \), what is \( y\!\left(\dfrac{[[n]]\pi}{2\cdot[[omega]]}\right) \)?
solution: Here \( c_1 = y(0) = [[y0]] \) and \( c_2 = y'(0)/[[omega]] = [[v0]]/[[omega]] \). At \( t = \dfrac{[[n]]\pi}{2\cdot[[omega]]} \), the phase is \( [[omega]]t = \dfrac{[[n]]\pi}{2} \), so \( y = [[y0]]\cos\!\left(\dfrac{[[n]]\pi}{2}\right) + \dfrac{[[v0]]}{[[omega]]}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[n]]\pi}{2}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of \( \cos \) and \( \sin \) in the general solution.
This ignores the velocity term and reports only \( y(0) \).
This ignores the position term and reports only the velocity contribution.
This flips the overall sign of the solution.
This flips the sign of the velocity contribution only.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
